narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like grades, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, LSAT score of 27, Year 1 school decile of 2, Year 3 decile of 1, middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted an 82.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their strong pass rates. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is well below the typical student's rank of around the 5th or 6th decile and signaled to the model a significant drop-off in performance compared to peers. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile of 2, also lower than the average of about the 6th decile, reinforcing concerns about your early academic standing relative to others in the dataset. The one factor that worked slightly in your favor was your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, which, while below the typical 3.21 average, nudged the prediction a bit toward passing success. Overall, your grades placed you in the lower deciles compared to most students who pass the bar, outweighing other elements like your LSAT or family income in the model's eyes. Your full-time status aligns with the vast majority of students (over 90%), so it didn't stand out as a differentiator. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male student—many from similar backgrounds excel—but the model saw your academic deciles as the biggest red flags when benchmarked against historical bar passers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the 37 average, improving your undergraduate record through post-bac courses if possible, or targeting schools with more holistic reviews that weigh personal stories alongside metrics. Building a stronger narrative around your resilience, perhaps through internships or leadership in Asian student networks, could also help in reapplying. With targeted improvements, you can shift these predictions in your favor next time.
